title: Electron-Photon Spatial Entanglement in Coherent Cathodoluminescence
authors: Tatsuro Yuge, Ryo Okamoto, Takumi Sannomiya, Keiichirou Akiba
abstract: Electron-photon quantum entanglement in an electron microscope paves the way for a new quantum platform, enabling the integration of quantum functionalities into electron microscopy and opening opportunities for quantum imaging and quantum sensing at the nanoscale. To realize such a platform, it is crucial to understand the degree and nature of electron-photon entanglement in cathodoluminescence (CL). However, its dependence on electron-beam properties, particularly transverse coherence, remains unclear. Here, we present a theoretical framework describing the quantum state of an electron-photon pair generated in coherent CL, specifically for transition radiation. By expressing the scattered state directly via the luminescence spectrum, we evaluate the entanglement using both subsystem purity and an Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen-type criterion. These two measures enable a clear distinction between wave-like, particle-like, and classical regimes in terms of spatial and momentum entanglement in the electron-photon system. Our analysis identifies the roles of the electron's transverse and longitudinal coherence, as well as the photon's spectral width, and reveals the conditions under which strong spatial entanglement emerges. This unified perspective clarifies the nature of electron-photon quantum correlations in coherent CL, leading to quantum-enabled functionalities in electron microscopy.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to quantify spatial entanglement between electrons and photons in coherent cathodoluminescence. It classifies the system into wave-like, particle-like, and classical regimes based on beam coherence and photon bandwidth. The findings provide a roadmap for using electron microscopy as a platform for quantum-enabled imaging and sensing.
main_problem: Quantifying the degree and nature of electron-photon spatial entanglement in coherent cathodoluminescence (CL) and determining how it depends on electron-beam properties like transverse coherence.
main_result: The study identifies three distinct entanglement regimes (wave-like, particle-like, and classical) and reveals that strong spatial entanglement emerges when the electron's transverse coherence length is large and the photon spectral width is optimized.
method: A theoretical framework that reconstructs the scattered electron-photon state from the luminescence spectrum and evaluates entanglement using subsystem purity and an EPR-type uncertainty criterion.
model_or_system: A single-electron beam interacting with a radiation field (specifically transition radiation) in a weak-coupling regime, modeled using a Hamiltonian for free electrons and photons with an interaction term.
key_observables: Subsystem purity (P_sc), EPR-type uncertainty product (D^2_sc), and joint probability distributions for position and wavevector.
important_parameters: Electron transverse and longitudinal coherence lengths (L_el_perp, L_el_parallel), photon spectral width (Delta_lambda_ph), and electron transverse wavenumber width (Delta_q_el_perp).
main_assumptions: The system is translationally invariant in the transverse plane, the interaction is in the weak-coupling regime, and the momentum change of the electron is much smaller than its incident momentum.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates the CL process; Fig 2 maps the entanglement regimes (A, B, C) against beam parameters; Fig 5 shows longitudinal entanglement via purity; Fig 6 shows joint probability distributions; Fig 7 examines the impact of phase uncertainty on the EPR criterion.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the interaction model and state reconstruction framework, proceeds to classify entanglement regimes using purity and EPR criteria, analyzes the influence of electron beam coherence, and concludes with an investigation into the impact of unknown phase factors.
why_interesting: It provides a bridge between electron microscopy and quantum optics by treating electron-photon scattering as a source of continuous-variable entanglement, relevant to quantum imaging and sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of coherent cathodoluminescence in transition radiation. The wave packet of an incident electron has central momentum ℏq0 in the z direction with the spatial widths Lel ⊥and Lel ∥in the transverse (xy) and longitudinal (z) directions, respectively. A photon in the radiation has a central wavenumber kc (wavelength λc = 2π/kc) in the direction of θ with the spectral width ∆λph. The electron undergoes a recoil to gain transverse momentum ℏq⊥.

FIG. 2. (a) Parameter regimes of entanglement in the scattered state |Ψsc⟩(Sec. III) of the electron–photon sys- tem. Regimes A (red), B (light green), and C (blue) are defined based on (b) subsystem purity Psc (Sec. IV A) and (c) uncertainty product D2 sc (Sec. IV B). Regime A corre- sponds to the wave-like entangled regime, where Psc < 2/3 and D2 sc < 1; Regime B to the particle-like entangled regime, where Psc < 2/3 and D2 sc > 1; and Regime C to the classical regime, where Psc > 2/3 and D2 sc > 1. The upper hatched re- gion indicates the entangled regime identified by the electron z-subsystem purity Pel z < 2/3, i.e., entanglement between the electron’s z-degree of freedom and the...

FIG. 3. Schematic illustrations of the x-directional wavevector-space functions in Eq. (17) shown for the three regimes labeled A, B, and C in Fig. 2(a). The upper panels depict ψ(x) ini (qx+kx) for (i) kx = k′ c+∆kph, (ii) kx = k′ c−∆kph, (iii) kx = −k′ c+∆kph, and (iv) kx = −k′ c −∆kph. The lower panels depict G(kx). (a) Regime A, where ∆qel ⊥≪∆kph. The functions ψ(x) ini (qx + kx) of qx for different kx are distinguishable within each of the two peaks of G(kx), which leads to nearly one-to-one correlation (represented by thin lines): for each photon with kx, a corresponding electron with qx = −kx is correlated. FIG. 4. Entanglement measures of the scattered state |Ψsc⟩ for different values of Lel ∥, plotted as functions of ∆qel ⊥and ∆kph. Left panels [(a) and (c)] show the results for the sub- system purity Psc, and right panels [(b) and (d)] show those for the uncertainty product D2 sc. (a) and (b): Results for Lel ∥= 0.13 µm (∆qel ∥≃48.3 µm−1). (c) and (d): Results for Lel ∥= 13 µm (∆qel ∥≃0.483 µm−1). The other parameter values are the same as those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Subsystem purity Pel z of the electron’s z-degree of freedom in the scattered state |Ψsc⟩as a function of the luminescence spectral width ∆kph. The dashed, solid, and dotted curves correspond to Lel ∥= 0.13, 1.3 and 13 µm (∆qel ∥≃48.3, 4.83 and 0.483 µm−1), respectively. The other parameter values are the same as those in Fig. 2.
